coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of electrode properties using carbon dots functionalized magnetite nanoparticles for azo dye decolorization in microbial fuel cell
coredata.dc:creator: Surti, Parini
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
Mungray, Arvind Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Technology integration of nanomaterials with microbial fuel cell (MFC) have led to simultaneous degradation of recalcitrant dyes and energy extraction from textile wastewater. Limited electron transfer capacity and hydrophobicity of electrode are the bottlenecks for enhancing the performance of MFC. Nanomaterials can provide surface functionalities for electron transfers and serve as catalyst for pollutant degradation. In this paper, magnetite nanoparticles functionalized with carbon dots (Fe3O4@CDs) were used to enhance the electron transfer capacity of the electrodes due to numerous surface-active functional groups of CDs and the reversible redox reaction of Fe2+/Fe3+. Polydopamine (PDA) was used as binder to coat Fe3O4@CDs onto the surface of carbon felt (CF) electrodes in a sono-chemical reaction, favoring to form biocompatible electrodes. Charge transfer resistance of Fe3O4@CDs@PDA-CF was 5.02Ω as compared to 293.34Ω of unmodified CF. Fe3O4@CDs@PDA-CF installed MFC could achieve almost 98% dye degradation efficiency within 48 h and 18.30 mW m−2 power output as compared to 77% dye degradation and 0.34 mW m−2 power output by unmodified CF electrode MFC. Moreover, metagenomic analysis of microbial consortia developed in Fe3O4@CDs@PDA-CF MFC showed enrichment of electrogenic and dye degrading microbial communities of Achromobacter. Delftia, Geobacter and Pseudomonas.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Technology integration of nanomaterials with microbial fuel cell (MFC) have led to simultaneous degradation of recalcitrant dyes and energy extraction from textile wastewater. Limited electron transfer capacity and hydrophobicity of electrode are the bottlenecks for enhancing the performance of MFC. Nanomaterials can provide surface functionalities for electron transfers and serve as catalyst for pollutant degradation. In this paper, magnetite nanoparticles functionalized with carbon dots (Fe3O4@CDs) were used to enhance the electron transfer capacity of the electrodes due to numerous surface-active functional groups of CDs and the reversible redox reaction of Fe2+/Fe3+. Polydopamine (PDA) was used as binder to coat Fe3O4@CDs onto the surface of carbon felt (CF) electrodes in a sono-chemical reaction, favoring to form biocompatible electrodes. Charge transfer resistance of Fe3O4@CDs@PDA-CF was 5.02Ω as compared to 293.34Ω of unmodified CF. Fe3O4@CDs@PDA-CF installed MFC could achieve almost 98% dye degradation efficiency within 48 h and 18.30 mW m−2 power output as compared to 77% dye degradation and 0.34 mW m−2 power output by unmodified CF electrode MFC. Moreover, metagenomic analysis of microbial consortia developed in Fe3O4@CDs@PDA-CF MFC showed enrichment of electrogenic and dye degrading microbial communities of Achromobacter. Delftia, Geobacter and Pseudomonas.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rising concern of water crises has pushed the research towards finding sustainable solutions to treat wastewater. Hazardous pollutants discharged by industrial effluents into natural water bodies have climbed up the ecological pyramid causing significant damage at each trophic level. Dye compounds are a major class of pollutants released from textile, printing, tanneries and food processing industries (Shen et al., 2022). Dyes inhibits light penetration in water bodies, cause oxygen depletion, impairs photosynthesis in plants and have mutagenic effect to the life forms if released untreated (Lellis et al., 2019; Karimi et al., 2022). Azo dyes, characterized by the presence of characteristic azo group (-NN-), are major class of dyes used for textile dyeing and at times as food color (Alizadeh et al., 2022). 10–15% of the azo dyes used in coloring process is wasted as excess flow through altering the pH and increasing the chemical oxygen demand (COD) of the textile effluent (Sarkar et al., 2017; Khan et al., 2022). Conventional treatment technologies are cost intensive, have a significant carbon footprint and seldom achieve effluent treatment standards (Oladoja, 2017). Hence, sustainable and cost-effective treatment technologies are required that can efficiently harvest energy or recover resources from such dye containing wastewater.

Microbial fuel cell (MFC) is an electrochemical system in which organic content in the wastewater is oxidized by microbes and generated electrons are harvested using electrodes (Logan, 2008). There have been several reports for treatment of dye containing wastewater at either anode or cathode of MFC (Mani et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2021). At anode, electrons released from microbial metabolism may reduce the azo bond indirectly with redox mediator or directly with membrane bound azoreductase (Mani et al., 2019). Alternatively, azo dyes can also be reduced in the cathodic reaction (Ma et al., 2021). However, successful implementation of MFC as textile wastewater treatment technology suffers from several bottlenecks like low power output due to MFC internal resistances, low treatment capacity and high hydraulic retention times for efficient treatment (Holkar et al., 2016). Various modifications like integration of MFC with existing treatment technology, electrode modification, membrane modification as well as variation in MFC configuration have been employed for improving MFC efficiency (Pant et al., 2012).

Electrodes are the significant component of MFC as it houses the microbial catalyst and forms the conductive channel for electron transfer from microbial metabolism to the external circuit (Pant et al., 2012). Electrodes in MFC must have certain characteristics like biocompatibility, large surface area, low charge and mass transfer resistance, non-corrosive, non-toxic and should be cheap and easily accessible. Graphite rod, carbon felt (CF), carbon fibres, carbon paper and carbon cloth are some of the routinely used electrode materials in the MFC (Mustakeem, 2015). However, bare carbon surfaces are hydrophobic and have high charge transfer resistance, which minimizes the performance of MFC. Modification of carbon electrodes by coating with conductive polymers, functionalization with nanomaterials and immobilization of electroactive microbes have been studied to improve electrode performance in MFC (Chiranjeevi and Patil, 2020; Hindatu et al., 2017).

Nanostructure materials have recognized as a promising class of materials for remarkable applications in multidisciplinary research areas. They can be prepared from organic precursors, metal oxides, nitrides and metal organic hybrid nanocomposites. Ease of synthesis, green chemistry and low cost of nanomaterials have put them in research highlight for varied applications (Baig et al., 2021). The special features of nanomaterials include higher surface area, thermal and electrical conductivity, mechanical strength, tunable optical properties and desired surface chemistry. Besides, the surface of the nanomaterials can be functionalized to further fine tune these features. These materials have been endowed with various functionalities like pollutants and heavy metal detection (Sanko et al., 2022; Sohrabi et al., 2021); adsorption and degradation of contaminants (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2020; Karaman et al., 2022; Fazli et al., 2022); waste valorization (Orooji et al., 2022a); electrochemical (Tümay et al., 2022; Orooji et al., 2021) and optical sensors (Orooji et al., 2020); resource recovery (Orooji et al., 2022b); fuel cell application and super capacitors (Arun et al., 2019); and biomedical applications such as drug delivery and cell imaging (Tiwari et al., 2019; Mitchell et al., 2020).

Carbon dots (CDs) derived from cheap carbon containing materials is a novel class of functional material consisting of numerous surface-active functional groups that can undergo reversible redox reactions, which acts as a shuttle for transferring of electrons. CDs fed Shewanella oneidensis showed enhanced biofilm formation and current output (6.46-fold) in MFC (Yang et al., 2020). CDs have also been shown to act as electron shuttles as suspension in anode enhancing power density of MFC by 22.5% (Vishwanathan et al., 2016). N-doped CDs have been prepared from lignite and coated onto the surface of CF to prepare supercapacitor electrodes (Dharmalingam et al., 2017). CDs chelated with metal ions have been shown to improve the oxygen reduction reaction at cathode in MFC and increase the power density (Loukanov et al., 2019). CDs derived from candle soot were used for surface modification of electrodes over stainless steel backbone for MFC which reached up-to 1650 mW/m2 power density (Singh et al., 2018).

Magnetic (Fe3O4) nanoparticles can be prepared by a simple co-precipitation reaction with Fe2+/Fe3+ ions under alkaline conditions. Functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles have versatile applications in biosensors, contaminant degradation, biomedical application and wastewater purification (Lee et al., 2018; Wahab et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2018). Self-propelling microbots were prepared by coating kitchen waste derived CDs over Fe3O4 nanoparticles to degrade dye from wastewater (Shivalkar et al., 2021). Fe3O4@calciumlignosulfonate@diamine@Cu nanocomplex was shown to act as photocatalyst for dye degradation and could be recycled up to five times (Orooji et al., 2022a). Functional CDs-Ag-Fe3O4 nanocomposite was prepared as a nanocatalyst for the reduction of organic dyes (Zahedifar et al., 2020). Fe@Fe2O3 N-doped CDs coated anode in MFC was shown to boost electricity generation by enrichment of exoelectrogens attaining up to 836 mW/m2 power density (Habibi et al., 2021).

Water hyacinth (WH) is an aquatic weed that doubles itself within a short span of 15 days covering the water body and depleting the oxygen and sunlight penetration. However, it can serve as substrate material for preparation of functional carbon composites and CDs. Magnetic carbon composite electrodes with supercapacitor and adsorbent properties were prepared from WH by carbonization in presence of Fe2+ ions (Saning et al., 2019). WH CDs and graphitic C3N4 nanocomposite acted as catalyst for efficient photodegradation of dichlorophenol (Hak et al., 2020). Mussel inspired coating of polydopamine (PDA) has been used for coating of various substrates to increase biocompatibility and hydrophilicity. PDA coated graphitic carbon foam has been used as anode in MFC for efficient promotion of biofilm formation, which enhances the power density by 6.7 times than MFC with CF anode (Jiang et al., 2017). PDA coating of Shewanella xiamenensis improved the electron transfer capacity in MFC owing to redox properties of PDA as well as by adsorbing flavin electron shuttle (Liu et al., 2019).

Considering the above, the aim of this work was to modify the surface of CF anode with Fe3O4@CDs@PDA for MFC with superior charge transfer potential, surface functionalities, increased surface area and enhanced hydrophilicity to promote electrogenic biofilm formation. Although there are many reports on photocatalytic dye degradation by CDs, direct use of CDs for anode modification to degrade azo dye bismark brown Y (BBY) in MFC has not been studied yet. Vishwanathan and team studied methylene blue decolorization by CDs to demonstrate their electrochemical activity in dye reduction-based electron transfer activity monitoring (DREAM) assay and further used CDs as suspension in MFC anolyte (Vishwanathan et al., 2016). WH derived CDs loaded on Fe3O4 nanoparticles with PDA binder have never been used as anode modification strategy for MFC assisted dye decolorization so far, to the best of our knowledge. Besides, most coating of MFC electrodes are reported by dip coating, spray coating or electrochemical coating of nanomaterials or polymers to achieve a homogenous layer of the conductive matrix on the electrode surface. Here, a facile method was adopted for surface modification of CF electrode with Fe3O4@CDs@PDA. In order to investigate the role of surface modified CF anode, electrochemical performance of CF electrodes with various nanocomposites (CDs@PDA, Fe3O4@PDA and Fe3O4@CDs@PDA) were studied in MFC. Fe3O4@CDs@PDA modified CF electrode was able to reach maximum power density of 18.30 mW m−2 which is superior than that of other nanocomposites modified CF electrodes. The results demonstrate the synergic behavior of Fe3O4@CDs@PDA modified CF electrode for efficient performance of MFC for dye degradation in textile wastewater.

All the chemicals used were of analytical grade. FeCl3 (98%), NH3 solution (25%), HNO3 (69–72%), CH3COONa (99%), NaHCO3 (99.8%), KH2PO4 (99%), NH4Cl (99%), CaCl2·2H2O (97%) and MgSO4·7H2O (99%) were purchased from Rankem Chemicals, India. Dopamine-HCl (98%), Tris aminomethane (99.8%), K3FeCN6 (99%), Bismarck brown Y (dye content 50%) were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Thioglycolate agar was purchased from Himedia Labs, India. CF and carbon fibre was obtained from a commercial supplier (Vinpro Technologies, Hyderabad, India). Dialysis membrane-70 was obtained from Himedia Labs for CDs purification. Nafion (Nafion 117, DuPont C., USA) was used as a proton exchange membrane in MFC setup.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized by chemical co-precipitation method (Petcharoen and Sirivat, 2012). FeCl3 and Fe2SO4 (2Fe3+:1Fe2+ ratio) were dissolved in distilled water, 30% (v/v) of NH3 solution was added drop-wise and stirred at 70 °C for 120 min, forming Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The as-prepared Fe3O4 were filtered, washed and dried.

WH (scientific name: Eicchornia crassipes) were collected from local Gavier lake, Surat, Gujarat, India. Roots of water hyacinth were cut from mainstem, washed, dried and powdered for CDs synthesis. 1.5 g of WH root powder was dispersed in 40 mL distilled water and autoclaved (Amar Equipment, India) at 200 °C for 8 h. Collected supernatant was dialysed against distilled water, characterized and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were functionalized with CDs (Fe3O4@CDs) using hydrothermal process (Zhang et al., 2010). In short, 200 mg of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 35 mL distilled water using ultrasonic bath (Labman, India). 5 mL of CDs solution was added to this dispersion and autoclaved at 200 °C for 4 h. Black precipitates of Fe3O4@CDs obtained were filtered and dried at 70 °C.

CF obtained from a commercial supplier was washed with acetone to remove any organic impurities and cut into desired electrode size. It was refluxed in 50% HNO3 for 1 h at 100 °C, neutralized using repeated distilled water wash and dried overnight at 70 °C. A facile method for coating the nanomaterial over CF electrodes was used. Dopamine is known to polymerize under alkaline conditions forming mussel inspired hydrophilic PDA layer on any substrate (Youn et al., 2019). Ultrasonication assisted method was employed for coating of acid treated CF electrodes (Rastgoo et al., 2016). In short, CF electrodes were dipped in dopamine solution (2 mg mL−1) prepared in Tris buffer (pH 8.5) and kept in an ultrasonic bath for 1 h. For coating of CDs@PDA, Fe3O4@PDA and Fe3O4@CDs@PDA; CDs solution, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Fe3O4@CDs were added to the respective dopamine polymerization mixture. Treated electrodes were washed twice with distilled water and dried at 70 °C.

Absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded with Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA) and Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was done with Alpha II (Bruker, Germany). Surface morphology of the electrodes was analysed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi S3400 M). Hydrophobicity of the prepared electrodes was measured by water contact angle test (DataPhysics OCA, Germany).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) of the electrodes was done in 0.1 M phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.4) in a three-electrode setup with Ag/AgCl as reference, CF as working and Pt as counter electrode on potentiostat/galvanostat system (CH Instruments, 608C). CV measurements were done at the scan rate of 100 mV s−1.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was done in three electrodes setup with Ag/AgCl as a reference, CF as working and Pt as counter electrode on potentiostat/galvanostat system (CH Instruments, 608C) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). EIS measurements were taken at open circuit potential (OCP) over the frequency range of 1–100,000 Hz at potential amplitude of 0.01 V. Charge transfer resistance was determined from diameter of semicircle in Nyquist plot. Electrode coating with lowest charge transfer resistance was used for MFC setup.

Typical H shaped dual chambered glass MFC with a working volume of 100 mL each for anode and cathode; separated by Nafion membrane was used to validate the performance of treated anode in MFC. Bare CF MFC consisted of bare CF anode and treated CF MFC consisted of Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF anode. In both bare CF MFC and treated CF MFC, carbon felt with dimensions 4.5 × 2.5 cm2 and 15 cm carbon fibre cathode were used (Arkatkar et al., 2021). Anolyte consisted of synthetic wastewater (sodium acetate 1 g/L, NaHCO3 24 mg/L, KH2PO4 14 mg/L, NH4Cl 60 mg/L, CaCl2. 2H2O 18 mg/L, MgSO4·7H2O 24 mg/L (Pardeshi and Mungray, 2014); amended with BBY (100 mg/L). Potassium ferricyanide (100 mM) was used as electron acceptor in the catholyte consisting PBS (100 mM, pH 7.0). Anaerobic sludge from textile effluent treatment plant (Palsana Enviro Protection Pvt. Ltd.) was used to inoculate the anolyte at 5% v/v. MFCs was purged with N2 gas to maintain the anaerobic conditions. All the MFCs were operated in open circuit mode to acclimatize the microbial community after which the circuit was completed using 1200 Ω external resistor. MFCs were operated in fed batch mode; fresh acetate (1 g/L) was fed after every 48 h and anolyte was changed after 96 h. Chemical oxygen demand (COD) of anolyte was measured before and after BBY decolorization in MFC as per American public health association (APHA) standard methods. All MFC sets were run in duplicates to verify the reproducibility of data.

Open and closed-circuit potentials were constantly monitored using data acquisition system (34972 A, Keysight Technologies, USA). Current density was calculated using the formula:

where F is Faraday's constant, Vanode is the volume of anolyte and ΔCOD is the change in COD over time t of operation of MFC (Logan, 2008).
CV and EIS of all the MFCs was done in a three electrodes setup with Ag/AgCl as reference, anode as working and cathode as counter electrode on potentiostat/galvanostat system (CH Instruments, 608C).

Microbial enrichment was monitored by measuring colony forming units (CFU) per mL by spreading serially diluted initial inoculum and anolyte sample on thioglycolate agar plates. SEM analysis of the electrode surface was done to visualize biofilm formation over anode surface. Electrodes were overnight incubated in glutaraldehyde (2.5%, v/v) and then sequentially dehydrated in ethanol (20–100%, v/v) for fixing microbes onto electrode surface for SEM analysis.

Dye decolorization was monitored by measuring the absorbance of anolyte at 457 nm (absorbance maxima of BBY) using UV–Visible spectrophotometer (HACH DR6000). Percentage decolorization was calculated by formula:

where Ab(initial) and Ab(final) are the initial and final absorbance of anolyte at 457 nm respectively. Moreover, complete wavelength scan from 200 to 600 nm was done to validate the BBY decolorization and conversion to uncoloured aromatic amines.

Anode and anolyte samples from bare and treated CF MFC were used for 16 S rRNA sequencing to analyse the microbial community developed in respective MFC. Anode biofilm was scrapped from the electrodes and washed in normal saline. Anolyte was centrifuged to collect the microbial cells followed by normal saline wash. DNA was extracted using soil DNA extraction kit, followed by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) using primers for V3–V4 region of 16 S rRNA gene. Additional 8 cycles of PCR were done using Illumina barcoded adapters. Sequencing was done using Illumina Miseq sequencer. Obtained sequences were analysed using Galaxy platform (Afgan et al., 2016).

WH is a troublesome aquatic weed which has a high reproductive rate and completely covers the water body in a short time. It contains a significant amount of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin which makes it an ideal source for synthesis of functional carbon materials (Saning et al., 2019). Hydrothermal carbonization was used to convert this waste into CDs to prepare nano-functionalized electrodes for MFC application. Scheme for carbon dots preparation from WH root powder is as shown in Fig. 1 a.

The hydrothermally prepared CDs were dialyzed against water to obtain homogenous suspension of CDs. Fig. 1b shows the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the prepared carbon dots. WH CDs showed characteristic absorbance at 320 nm which confirmed the presence of π-π* and n-π* transition states due to the existence of various surface-active functional groups like CC, CN, CO and C–O. The as-prepared WH CDs exhibited emission peak at 410 nm when excited at 330 nm. Visible blue fluorescence was observed upon excitation with UV-lamp at 365 nm as shown in Fig. 1a.

Magnetic (Fe3O4) nanoparticles prepared by chemical co-precipitation were functionalized with the CDs by hydrothermal method. Surface active functional groups were analysed for the prepared CDs, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Fe3O4@CDs using FT-IR spectroscopy (as shown in Fig. 1c). WH CDs showed characteristic peaks at 3422, 1620, 1403, 1104 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of –OH, CO, C–NH–C and C–N/C–S functional groups respectively. This FT-IR spectrum strongly suggests that CDs with multiple surface functionalities were successfully formed. The FT-IR spectrum for Fe3O4 particles showed peaks corresponding to –OH stretching at 3408 cm−1 and characteristic peaks for Fe–O stretch at 630 cm−1. The FTIR spectrum of Fe3O4@CDs showed lower peak intensity corresponding to –OH functional group as compared to CDs, indicating the attachment of WH CDs onto the surfaces of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Surface –COOH group of CDs, bonds with Fe in Fe3O4 and weakens the CO bond stretching, implying the utilization of CO bond for surface functionalization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with CDs. It can be observed that peaks corresponding to C–N/C–S and C–NH–C stretching in Fe3O4@CDs have lower peak intensity as compared to CDs, further confirming the functionalization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Further, intense peak corresponding to Fe–O stretching can be observed for Fe3O4@CDs.

Ultrasonication assisted method was used for coating the CF electrodes. The extreme temperature and pressure created during the cavitation using ultrasonic waves, provides sufficient energy for functionalization of CF with the nanomaterials. Further, the free radicals generated due to bubble collapse oxidizes the CF surface and microjets induced in the process project the nanomaterial onto the CF surface resulting in strong adherence of nanoparticles on CF (Rastgoo et al., 2016). Coating of CF was confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1d). Bare CF showed no significant IR absorbance. HNO3 treated CF showed intense peaks at 3436 and 1627 cm−1 corresponding to –OH and CO groups of CF. This indicates increased density of oxygen containing functional groups on the CF surface corresponding to increase in polarity and wettability by acidic treatment (Tiwari and Bijwe, 2014). CF PDA showed characteristic peaks at 3276, 1375, 1250 cm−1 represent he stretching of aromatic –OH/–NH, –C-H, phenolic C–O–H groups of polydopamine (Liu et al., 2019). CF CDs@PDA showed intense peaks at 3415, 3240, 1627 cm−1 corresponding to –OH, phenolic –OH/-NH, CO functional groups, confirming CF functionalization with CDs and PDA. FT-IR spectrum of CF Fe3O4@PDA showed peaks for –OH (3436 cm−1), aromatic –OH/-NH (3234 cm−1), CO (1627 cm−1), 1048 (C–O) and Fe–O (594 cm−1). The FT-IR spectrum of CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA showed peaks corresponding to all the functional groups present in PDA, CDs and Fe3O4. Particularly intense peak appears at 3234 cm−1 corresponding to –NH which indicates increased abundance of positive charged groups on the CF as compared to other coated CF. Positive charge distribution over CF surface helps to reduce electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged cell walls of bacterial cells, aiding faster biofilm formation on CF. Positive charge distribution on the electrode surface also aids in capturing electrons resulting in higher charge transfers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CF electrodes by SEM and water contact angle measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The surface coating of CF was analysed using SEM. It can be observed in Fig. 2 a that bare CF has a smooth surface and Fig. 2b shows Fe3O4@CDs@PDA coated CF have rough surface, revealing the successful deposition of Fe3O4@CDs@PDA on the surface of CF. Moreover, the acid treatment increased the surface etching and overall active surface area increased for the coated CF electrodes.

Contact angle measurement was done to confirm the hydrophobicity of the as prepared electrode. Fig. 2c shows the water droplet contact angle on bare CF (104.5°), CF HNO3 (91.7°) and PDA coated CF (0°). PDA significantly increased the wettability of the CF as can be observed by 0° water contact angle on coated CF surface. Since, all coated electrodes had PDA deposition; each became more hydrophilic (not shown for individual coated electrodes). Improved wettability of the electrodes corresponds to better bacterial loading and mass transfer onto the electrodes which can facilitate higher charge transfer in MFC (Jiang et al., 2017).

The CV curve of bare CF, CF HNO3, CF PDA, CF CDs@PDA, CF Fe3O4@PDA and CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA is as shown in Fig. 3 a. PBS was used as an electrolyte to analyse the redox capacity of the electrodes as it can best mimic the biological conditions. K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 in supporting KCl electrolyte is already known to undergo rapid redox conversion on cyclic sweeping of the potential. In absence of a redox active couple like Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4− in electrolyte, any redox peak can be attributed to the redox active moiety coated on the electrodes. It can be observed that CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA shows higher current density compared to all other coated electrodes. Bare CF electrode showed negligible redox activity due to absence of any redox active groups and low surface area. Further, CF HNO3 showed slight improvement over bare CF due to the presence of –OH groups. Maximum current density recorded by electrodes was in the order CF PDA < CF CDs@PDA < CF Fe3O4@PDA < CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA. Since, most functional groups as well as Fe+2/Fe+3 redox couple are present on CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA; it has more redox active moieties giving higher current density in CV. It is worth noting that the CV curve of CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA also represents the maximum available electrode surface area for electron transfer amongst all coated electrodes.

Nyquist plots obtained for coated and uncoated electrodes are as shown in Fig. 3b. The semicircle diameter in the Nyquist plot represents the interfacial resistance across the electrode-electrolyte interface. Bare CF showed significant mass transfer resistance due to its high hydrophobicity. Treatment of CF with HNO3 has exfoliated surface and presence of more oxygenated functional groups on surface which improved the interfacial charge transfer as compared to bare CF. PDA significantly improved the hydrophilic behaviour of the CF, hence all the coated electrodes showed better diffusion properties and lower charge transfer impedance as compared to uncoated electrodes. As it can be observed from the Nyquist plot, the charge transfer resistance decreases in order CF HNO3 (293.34 Ω) > CF PDA (148.28 Ω) > CF CDs@PDA (77.46 Ω) > CF Fe3O4@PDA (57.35 Ω) > CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA (5.02 Ω). CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA shows the minimum charge transfer resistance across the interface which can be correlated to the presence of more redox active sites and larger available surface area across the electrode. The straight line obtained at lower frequencies in the plot correlates to the diffusion of the reactants and products towards the electrode surface. It is worth noting that CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA has a much steeper line at lower frequencies as compared to other coated electrodes indicating better mass transfer inside the electrode pores. Hence, EIS spectra confirms that the CF Fe3O4@CDs@PDA has the potential to improve the electron transfer rates in MFC.

Fig. 4 a shows the graph of open circuit potential (OCP) of MFC with respect to time. As can be seen from the graph, there was an initial decrease in OCP for the first 18 h in both bare and treated CF MFC corresponding to the adaptation of microbes to the MFC environment and given culture conditions (Tacas et al., 2021). Post this adaptation period, the OCP started increasing as the microbes entered the log phase. Treated CF MFC reached a stable OCP of 360 mV while bare CF MFC reached maximum OCP of 340 mV then showed a sharp decline in OCP which might be due to depletion of C source. Treated CF MFC was stable during this period indicating the capacitive nature of the anode in MFC. Acetate was fed in the system at 48 h, which resulted in increase in OCP of both the MFC. However, the peak OCP obtained during the second feeding cycle did not increase much due to the accumulation of aromatic amines which might limit the microbial metabolism due to its toxicity (Ng et al., 2017). Complete anolyte was changed at 96 h which resulted in increase of OCP in bare CF MFC. By 4th feeding cycle microbes in both the MFC were acclimatized to the dye concentration and its degradation products and reached a stable OCP. Maximum OCP achieved in 200 h by treated CF MFC was 721 mV which was 1.2 folds higher than OCP achieved by bare CF MFC of 582 mV. Moreover, the treated CF MFC showed a stable and constant increase in OCP without any decline indicating stable biofilm formation over treated anode where only acetate was the limiting factor. When acetate was replenished, more electrons were generated and accumulated on the electrode. Treated CF has more redox active sites to store charge resulting in higher OCP than bare CF under given acetate concentration.

Most of the studies on dye decolorization using MFC, report an acclimatization phase for microbial community in MFC reactor and once, a stable voltage output is reached, azo dye is added (Cui et al., 2019; Quan et al., 2018; Zhong et al., 2018). Dyes tend to have toxic effects on microbes which may decrease the voltage output in MFC. In this study, microbial community was enriched in presence of BBY from the start of the reactor to create a selective pressure on the microbes for dye decolorization as well as electron transfer to anode. Microbes generally require a co-substrate for azo dye decolorization as dyes cannot be directly utilized as C source. In few studies, nutrient rich co-substrates like yeast extract or Luria broth have been reported to aid decolorization efficiency since more electrons can be made available for reduction to aromatic amines (Tacas et al., 2021). Acetate was used as co-substrate in this study, which resulted in slow but steady increase in OCP in MFC and enrichment of dye decolorizing community in minimal nutrient medium.

After a stable OCP was reached, both the MFC circuits were closed with external load to allow electrons to flow from anode to cathode. Bare CF MFC exhibited maximum power density of 0.34 mW m−2 in response to acetate addition and anolyte change. While treated CF MFC showed a continuous increase in power density reaching a maximum of 18.30 mW m−2. The maximum coulombic efficiency of bare CF MFC was 0.21% while treated CF MFC showed a maximum coulombic efficiency of 5.81%. Coulombic efficiency is the measure of electrons extracted from microbial metabolism for electricity generation. Low CE is achieved in MFC treating azo dye since electrons are utilized for supporting microbial growth and dye reduction. However, treated CF MFC had improved CE (27 times more than bare CF MFC) which can be attributed to enhanced electron extraction by treated CF anode. These results show that Fe3O4@CDs@PDA anode can achieve higher MFC power output by continuous oxidation of organic substrate due to enrichment of the electroactive community.

Change in current density was analysed in response to cyclic sweeping of voltage from −1.0 V – 0.5 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The area covered by the CV curve gives an idea of the charge transfer in MFC. Fig. 4b shows the CV curve of bare and treated CF MFC. The shape of the CV curve is representative of the microbial community present in the MFC and it can be observed that bare and treated CF MFC have different shapes. Microbial consortium enriched over the anode and in the anolyte may contribute to both direct and redox shuttle mediated electron transfers (Arkatkar et al., 2019). The CV of Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC has larger and wider redox peaks as compared to bare CF MFC. The rectangular shape of the treated CF MFC CV represents the capacitive nature of the biofilm developed over the anode. This indicates efficient electron transfer over treated CF surface as well as stable biofilm development over treated CF MFC. The peak current obtained at different voltages in Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated and bare CF MFC indicates the difference in catalytic sites present on both the CF surface. The redox moieties present on the treated CF MFC reduces the activation losses of the treated CF anode resulting in higher current densities for the same concentration of substrate.

EIS curve was obtained for both bare and Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC at OCP and amplitude range of 1–100,000 Hz. Fig. 4c shows the Nyquist plot of bare and treated CF MFC. Resistance offered by anolyte was almost similar in both treated CF (15.62 Ω) and bare CF (13.42 Ω) MFC. Since no external redox mediator was added and anolyte from both MFC had the same composition, it had similar electrolyte resistance. The charge transfer resistance in treated CF MFC as calculated from semicircle diameter was 36.57 Ω while charge transfer resistance in bare CF MFC was 109.08 Ω. Significant mass transfer resistance was observed in both the MFC as both the reactors were operated in static mode without any stirring or flow of anolyte. These results confirmed the superior charge transfer properties of Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC and explains the higher power densities obtained with it.

Anolyte was withdrawn from bare and treated CF MFC after 48 h, serially diluted and plated on thioglycolate agar plates to count the CFU/mL. Sludge inoculum had 5 × 104 CFU/mL which increased to 6 × 107 CFU/mL in bare CF MFC and 2 × 108 CFU/mL in treated CF MFC. Anaerobic condition was maintained in the anode compartment of both MFC by purging N2 gas. The only electron acceptors for driving microbial metabolism in MFC anode chamber were either electrodes or BBY dye. Treated CF anode had several surface-active functional groups from CDs as well as Fe2+/Fe3+ redox couple to accept electrons from microbial metabolism. Hence, a faster growth rate was observed in Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC. Besides, electroactive microbes are known to efficiently utilize Fe ions as electron acceptors (Yee et al., 2020). PDA coated surfaces are known to be biocompatible and enhance microbial attachment. Hence, a large number of microbes adhered to treated CF anode as compared to bare CF surface as depicted in SEM image of electrodes post 48 h of inoculation (Fig. S1). Microcolonies of bacteria could be visualized in the SEM image of treated CF indicating initiation of biofilm formation.

Planktonic microbes generally transfer electrons in MFC via indirect electron transfer while biofilm microbes can directly transfer electrons due to physical contact between their cell membrane and anode (Arkatkar et al., 2021). These results showed increase in cell density of both planktonic cells as well as those adhering to the anode in treated CF MFC. These results confirm that Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated anode reduces the start-up time of MFC by encouraging faster metabolism and growth of electroactive microbes. Faster growth can also be correlated with higher OCP achieved in treated CF MFC.

BBY is a cationic diazo dye used in textiles and histology staining. BBY was used in the anolyte since the start-up of MFC for enrichment of dye decolorizing microbes. Azo dyes are characterized by the presence of characteristic NN bonds which requires anaerobic condition for reduction to aromatic amines. Decolorization of azo dyes is generally due to co-metabolism of microbial communities in MFC deriving energy from carbon substrate (Fernando et al., 2013). Dye decolorization in bare and Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC was 49.08 ± 1.43% and 76.13 ± 1.62% respectively post 48 h s of MFC start-up. This was mainly due to the toxic effect of dye on the microbial metabolism. However, in Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC, the microbial community adapted faster reaching 81.25 ± 1.74% decolorization within the first 96 h as compared to 66.10 ± 2.90% decolorization in bare CF MFC. Post the acclimatization period, 97.5 ± 1.26% dye decolorization could be achieved by Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC within 48 h s with complete anolyte change while bare CF MFC could achieve 77.65 ± 1.40% decolorization. This was also accompanied by increase in OCP of the Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC which indicates presence of dye decolorizing and electroactive microbial community in the anode compartment. Post the adaptation period, the dye decolorization remained fairly constant.

Azo dyes have absorption maxima in the visible region while its conversion to corresponding aromatic amines shifts the absorption maxima to ultraviolet region (Pushkar and Mungray, 2016). A complete wavelength scan from 200 to 600 nm was done to verify the conversion of BBY to corresponding aromatic amines (Fig. S2). As can be observed from the absorption spectra of anolyte, Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC shows increase in peak intensity around 220 nm which confirms the reduction of azo dyes to aromatic amines. Fe3O4 are known adsorbents for dye compounds and PDA creates a biocompatible surface for microbial adherence. Hence, Fe3O4@CDs@PDA brings dye compounds and microbial communities in close proximities for faster dye decolorization.

Dye decolorization and power generation both require efficient electron transfers which could be achieved by using treated CF electrodes in MFC. Besides, dye degradation products are also known to be efficient electron shuttles in MFC, which can further contribute to achieve higher power densities by withdrawal of electrons from metabolism of planktonic cells (Ng et al., 2017). Table 1 summarizes several studies for dye decolorization using coated anode materials in MFC. It is however difficult for different MFC studies to be compared with each other since different base anode materials of varying dimensions and porosity are used, different anolyte composition, variation in fed batch or continuous flow of anolyte, variation in cathode material and catholyte, variable distance between anode and cathode, difference in coating method and different dye compounds are used.

The 16 S rRNA metagenomic sequencing was carried out to gain insights into the microbial community developed in Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC and bare CF MFC. Table S1 demonstrates operational taxonomic units (OTUs) obtained from metagenomic sequencing data of the present study. Table 2 shows the distinct OTUs found in anode of bare and treated CF MFC. Alpha diversity was measured using Chao1, Shannon, Simpson and Fisher values to estimate the diversity of the sample communities. It was observed that the bare CF MFC anode had developed biofilm with greater microbial diversity as compared to Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC. Treated CF MFC showed selective enrichment of microbial communities. This can be attributed to the surface modification of the CF by Fe3O4@CDs@PDA which influenced the biofilm community of the anode.

The microbial composition based on phylum is as shown in Fig. S3. A total of 19 phyla were present in Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC and 23 phyla in bare CF MFC anolyte. The relative abundance of Proteobacteria, Firmicutes and Bacteroides in treated CF MFC were 86.69%, 1.70% and 5.71%; while in bare CF MFC anolyte were 34.49%, 22.05% and 15.70% respectively. Proteobacteria population are known for their metabolic flexibility and pollutant degradation. The known electrogenic genera of Geobacter, Shewanella and Pseudomonas also belong to this phylum. Increased abundance of Proteobacteria in MFC with enhanced dye degradation and higher power output is in agreement with the reported literature. Fusobacteria population has been reported in the microbial communities of effluent treatment plants treating dye containing industrial effluents (Pandit et al., 2021).

Table 3 shows the relative abundance of microbes at genera level which are known to be dye decolorizing and electrogenic. Achromobacter, Stenotrophomonas, Delftia and Brevunidmonas have been reported for diazo aye degradation (Mohan and v Vaishali, 2021; Zhu et al., 2018). Delftia and Stenotrophomonas have been reported for their electrode respiring properties as well (Jangir et al., 2016; Venkidusamy and Megharaj, 2016). Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC showed higher abundance of dye decolorizing and electrogenic genera. In summary, the microbial communities developed in treated CF MFC can be correlated with the higher power output and dye decolorization obtained by treated CF MFC.

A highly conductive and hydrophilic CF electrode was fabricated using sonication assisted deposition of Fe3O4@CDs@PDA over CF surface. The improvised surface chemistry of CF electrode decreased the charge transfer resistance and enhanced the electron transfer rate. PDA improved the hydrophilicity of CF electrode which promoted microbial adherence and faster biofilm growth. The maximum OCP achieved by treated CF MFC was 721 mV as compared to 582 mV OCP reached by bare CF MFC. Fe3O4@CDs@PDA treated CF MFC had lower activation losses resulting in stable power output over a longer period of time. The maximum power output, coulombic efficiency and dye decolorization achieved by treated CF MFC was 18.30 mW m−2, 5.81% and 98% respectively. Dye decolorization rate improved in treated CF MFC due to co-metabolism of electroactive and dye decolorizing communities enriched in MFC. Functionalized nanomaterials have the potential to improve the performance of MFC and treatment efficiency of wastewater. Such nanomaterial modified electrodes can be explored for scaling up of bio-electrochemical systems for increasing the performance of the system. Further, nanomaterials can also be explored for enrichment of the target specific microbial communities depending upon the surface functionalized groups.
